sections.title: The structural split
sections.content: India's IT sector is not collapsing it is bifurcating. The traditional IT services model, which absorbed hundreds of thousands of freshers for routine coding and testing work, is contracting. Meanwhile, Global Capability Centres (GCCs) of multinationals are building deep engineering teams in Bengaluru, Hyderabad, and Pune paying 30% more than legacy IT firms.

sections.title: Fresher pipeline
sections.content: Fresher hiring among tier-1 IT firms has fallen sharply from the FY22 peak of 6 lakh combined. Infosys remains the lone bright spot. Wipro and Tech Mahindra have deferred hundreds of joining letters. Campus hiring is recovering but concentrated at premier institutes and in AI/cloud roles.

sections.title: Where opportunities actually are
sections.content: GCCs are set to add 3 to 4 lakh net new jobs by 2026, with Hyderabad's continued focus on IT growth reinforcing this trend. India's startup ecosystem remains a strong hiring ground, evidenced by recent funding boosts like Cred's mega round which lifted weekly startup funding to $1.09 billion. The semiconductor mission targets 10 lakh jobs, and growing Tier-2 city IT hubs (Kochi, Coimbatore, Indore) offer lower competition. Mid-career developers with 3 to 7 years experience are in a seller's market.

sections.title: Skills that change your odds
sections.content: AI fluency, encompassing tools like GitHub Copilot and RAG pipelines, is increasingly vital across JDs, with Nasscom reaffirming India's key role in the global AI era. Demand for AI-skilled professionals is sustained, reflecting significant global investment and AI's broadening impact, including financial regulators developing AI tools. Cloud, DevOps, and data engineering roles are growing 7% YoY, while generic developer roles contract, making tailored keyword-optimised resumes mandatory as ATS thresholds rise to 80%.
